g.data-narration: Welcome to Parallel and Distributed Computing Paradigms - the foundation of modern cloud computing.
Cloud computing requires paradigms that handle geographic distribution, network latency, partial failures, and security across trust boundaries.
g.text: Cloud environments demand new programming models to handle:

g.data-narration: These paradigms provide structured methodologies for decomposing problems, distributing workloads, and coordinating execution across computing nodes.
Data parallelism applies the same operation to multiple data elements simultaneously, ideal for independent data segments like image processing.
g.text: Same operation applied to multiple data elements simultaneously

g.data-narration: Task parallelism executes different operations concurrently across processors, perfect for handling heterogeneous tasks like web server requests.
g.text: Different operations executed concurrently across processors

g.data-narration: Pipeline parallelism organizes computation as sequential stages where multiple data items flow through different stages simultaneously.
g.text: Sequential stages with multiple data items in different stages simultaneously

g.data-narration: The client-server model is fundamental where clients request services and servers provide them with centralized control and clear separation.
g.text: Most fundamental distributed paradigm - clients request, servers provide

g.data-narration: P2P creates decentralized networks where each node acts as both client and server, providing resilience and scalable resource sharing.
g.text: Decentralized networks - each node is both client and server

g.data-narration: MapReduce processes large datasets through three phases: Map creates key-value pairs, Shuffle groups them, and Reduce aggregates results.
The Actor Model treats actors as concurrent computation primitives that communicate through asynchronous messaging without shared state.
g.text: Actors communicate via asynchronous messages - no shared state

g.data-narration: MPI is a standardized message-passing system for parallel computing with point-to-point and collective operations across distributed memory.
g.text: Standardized message-passing for distributed memory systems

g.data-narration: The CAP theorem states distributed systems can only guarantee two of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
g.text: Distributed systems can guarantee only TWO of three properties

g.data-narration: Cloud services map to paradigms: Hadoop/Spark for MapReduce, AWS Lambda for serverless, Azure Functions for event-driven computing.
